title: Depression
lead: Depression isn't sadness you can talk yourself out of. It's a real, common condition that flattens mood, energy, and hope — and it can lift, especially with support.
explanation: Depression is more than feeling down. It's a persistent low mood or loss of interest that lasts weeks or longer and affects how you sleep, eat, think, and function. It can feel like heaviness, emptiness, or numbness rather than obvious sadness.
It often lies to you. Depression tells you that you're worthless, that nothing will change, that you're a burden. These are symptoms of the illness, not facts about you or your future.

Depression is not a character flaw or a failure of willpower. It involves changes in the brain and body, and it is one of the most treatable mental health conditions — through therapy, support, changes in daily life, and sometimes medication.
symptoms: Persistent low, empty, or numb mood
Thoughts that life isn't worth living

immediate.title: Do the next tiny thing
immediate.description: Depression makes big tasks impossible. Shrink it: not 'clean the house' but 'pick up one cup'. Not 'exercise' but 'stand outside for two minutes'. Momentum beats motivation.

immediate.title: Behavioural activation micro-step
steps: Pick one small activity that used to bring even mild pleasure or meaning
Do it for five minutes, even if you don't feel like it
Notice — without judging — how you feel afterwards

immediate.title: Reach one person
immediate.description: Send a single message to someone safe — even just 'having a hard day'. Connection is medicine, and you don't have to explain everything.

longTerm.title: Talk therapy
longTerm.description: CBT and behavioural activation have strong evidence for depression. They help you gently rebuild activity and challenge the hopeless stories depression tells.

longTerm.title: Rhythm and light
longTerm.description: Regular sleep and wake times, daylight in the morning, and gentle daily movement all help regulate the systems depression disrupts.

longTerm.title: Professional assessment
longTerm.description: A doctor or mental health professional can help you understand what's happening and discuss options, including whether medication might help. Asking for this is strength, not weakness.

resources.title: Depression — overview
resources.whyHere: Trustworthy plain-language explanation of symptoms and treatments.

resources.title: Depression
resources.whyHere: Global perspective and facts to counter the isolation depression creates.

resources.title: Behavioural activation self-help

resources.whyHere: Free training in awareness, connection and purpose — pillars that support recovery.

resources.whyHere: Free, anonymous emotional support from trained volunteer listeners, 24/7.

i have no energy for anything
